refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation approach) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and exclusion zones in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints); acquire raw spatial data; apply schema-driven extraction and validation; persist as a normalized model with audit trail; serve via standardized query API.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to autonomous agents (mobile platforms, drones) that operate under a pre-defined survey protocol; aggregate observations into a consensus model; validate against safety plan constraints; publish as a certified worksite model.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints); acquire raw spatial data; apply schema-driven extraction and validation; persist as a normalized model with audit trail; serve via standardized query API.

potential_sources: Schema evolution: Changes to feature definitions or safety attributes may require re-processing historical data; mitigate via versioned schema registry and backward-compatible transformations.

Acquisition method coupling: DP4 remains agnostic to sensing modality (vision, LiDAR, manual annotation) but assumes data arrives in a normalized intermediate form; risk if acquisition produces heterogeneous raw formats without pre-normalization.
Planning/execution interface coupling: DP4 depends on agreed query API contract; if downstream consumers require real-time streaming vs. batch snapshots, additional adapter layer may be needed.

Safety plan coupling: Feature schema must encode safety zone definitions and operational policies; tight coupling to safety plan evolution; mitigate via parameterized safety attributes and policy versioning.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves good separation of concerns: acquisition (input), schema-driven extraction (logic), persistence (storage), and query (output) are loosely coupled. Schema acts as a contract boundary. Risk is primarily in schema stability and downstream interface negotiation, not in core functional decomposition.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Raw spatial data (from sensors or manual input) can be normalized to a common intermediate representation before schema-driven extraction. Risk: If acquisition sources produce fundamentally incompatible formats, normalization cost may be high; recommend early data format audit.

Assumption: Feature schema is stable enough to support both current and near-term planning/execution use cases. Risk: Frequent schema changes indicate incomplete requirements; recommend stakeholder review of feature taxonomy and safety attributes before implementation.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formally documented and can be encoded as feature attributes and constraints. Risk: If policies are implicit or evolving, model validation will fail; recommend explicit policy capture and versioning.

Assumption: Planning/execution functions can tolerate snapshot-based model updates (not real-time streaming). Risk: If dynamic re-planning requires sub-second model freshness, DP4 may need augmentation with incremental update stream; recommend latency requirements clarification.

Assumption: Audit trail and model versioning are required for compliance/traceability. Risk: If not required, overhead of versioning may be unnecessary; recommend confirmation of regulatory/operational audit requirements.

Assumption: A single unified spatial coordinate frame is achievable and sufficient for all planning/execution functions. Risk: If different functions require different reference frames (e.g., local vs. global), additional transformation layer needed; recommend coordinate frame requirements analysis.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required feature types and attributes (e.g., obstacles, hazard zones, access points, equipment locations); (2) Spatial accuracy and update frequency requirements; (3) Query interface contract (e.g., 'retrieve all features within bounding box', 'get safety zone for location'); (4) Compliance/audit trail requirements. This will reduce ambiguity in schema design and downstream interface negotiation. Additionally, clarify whether DP4 should include real-time incremental updates or batch snapshot model; if real-time is required, consider hybrid approach (DP4 + streaming adapter). Validate that schema-driven extraction can handle heterogeneous acquisition sources without excessive pre-processing; if not, push normalization responsibility upstream to acquisition modules.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for declarative safety schema definition, spatial data acquisition and validation, normalized model persistence with audit trail, and standardized query API for safety-critical feature extraction and constraint enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound raw spatial data (geometry primitives, sensor readings, operational state) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for schema-driven extraction and validation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety constraint violations, audit trail events, and validated feature state queries to dependent consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for operational constraint queries (inbound) and constraint-aware task feasibility responses (outbound).

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: schema definition, validation logic, audit trail, and query API are all safety-domain concerns. Raw data acquisition via Telemetry interface is well-scoped. Minor diffusion: persistence layer may require coordination with external data store, but ownership of normalized model remains within Safety Services.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level orchestrator, not a data/schema management module. Placing schema, validation, and persistence here violates separation of concerns. Schema-driven extraction would diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation for actual data sourcing. Query API would require re-export through NGHE, creating unnecessary indirection.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational entity, not a data schema or validation authority. Safety schema and audit trail belong to a cross-cutting safety service, not vehicle-specific logic. Persistence and query API would be vehicle-centric, limiting reuse by Teleoperation and other consumers.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/interaction mode, not a data governance module. Schema definition and validation are orthogonal to teleoperation mechanics. Placing this DP here would couple safety schema to teleoperation-specific concerns and prevent Vehicle from directly accessing validated safety state.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of declarative safety schema, validation logic, normalized model persistence, and query API because: (1) safety attributes and constraints are core to its domain; (2) audit trail is a safety-critical requirement; (3) standardized query API enables Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution to consume validated safety state without reimplementing schema logic; (4) schema-driven extraction and validation are safety-domain responsibilities, not vehicle or teleoperation mechanics.
containment_risks: Data persistence layer (database, file store) may be external to Safety Services; ensure clear ownership boundary and interface contract for read/write operations.
Raw spatial data acquisition (Telemetry inbound) depends on Vehicle and Teleoperation to supply well-formed data; schema validation must be robust to malformed inputs.
Query API may become a bottleneck if high-frequency safety state checks are required; consider caching or event-driven notification patterns to reduce coupling.
Audit trail growth may require lifecycle management (archival, purging); ensure persistence strategy is documented and owned.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require 'worksite model/map generated and available to planning/execution functions for agreed set of site features.' Safety Services IM boundary directly satisfies this via standardized query API (outbound SafetyAlert interface) and normalized model persistence. Audit trail enables traceability of feature state changes, supporting compliance with 'Worksite safety plan and operational policies.' Verification can be measured by: (1) query API response time and completeness for agreed feature set, (2) audit trail coverage of all schema-driven extractions and validations, (3) availability of normalized model to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution consumers.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. Safety Services IM is the single, unambiguous owner of schema definition, validation, persistence, and query API. Interfaces are clearly defined: Telemetry (inbound raw data), SafetyAlert (outbound validated state and violations), Tasking (bidirectional constraint queries). No ambiguity about which module is responsible for maintaining the worksite model or enforcing safety constraints. Allocation is orthogonal to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution operational concerns.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 is complete and logically sound. It addresses all aspects of FR: (1) 'declarative feature schema' defines what spatial features are captured, (2) 'acquire raw spatial data' and 'schema-driven extraction' specify how data is sourced and interpreted, (3) 'validation' ensures conformance to safety attributes and operational constraints, (4) 'normalized model with audit trail' provides persistent, traceable representation, (5) 'standardized query API' makes model available to planning/execution functions. DP is expressed in functional/logical terms (not implementation-specific) and is independent of module packaging. Traceability to acceptance criteria is clear: normalized model with query API directly enables 'worksite model/map available to planning/execution functions'; audit trail supports 'Worksite safety plan and operational policies' compliance.

diffusion_risks: Persistence layer ownership: Database or file store for normalized model may be external to Safety Services (e.g., shared data warehouse, cloud storage). Ensure explicit interface contract (read/write semantics, consistency guarantees, latency SLAs) between Safety Services and persistence provider. Risk: unclear ownership of data lifecycle (archival, purging, backup) could lead to orphaned audit trail or stale model state.

Raw data acquisition robustness: Telemetry inbound interface depends on Vehicle and Teleoperation to supply well-formed geometry primitives and sensor readings. Schema validation must handle malformed, incomplete, or out-of-order inputs without corrupting normalized model. Risk: if validation logic is weak, invalid features could propagate to query API consumers, violating safety constraints.

Query API bottleneck: High-frequency safety state checks (e.g., real-time constraint validation during task execution) could saturate query API if implemented as synchronous request-response. Risk: latency could delay safety-critical decisions. Mitigation: consider event-driven notification (SafetyAlert outbound) or caching patterns to reduce coupling and improve responsiveness.

Audit trail lifecycle: Audit trail growth is unbounded if not managed. Ensure persistence strategy includes archival, purging, or retention policies. Risk: unbounded growth could degrade query API performance or exceed storage capacity, compromising availability of current safety state.

Schema evolution: Declarative schema may need to evolve as worksite features or safety policies change. Ensure schema versioning and backward compatibility are defined. Risk: unmanaged schema changes could invalidate existing audit trail or break query API contracts with consumers.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services IM achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Excellent fit: schema definition, validation logic, audit trail, and query API are all safety-domain concerns and are cohesively owned within Safety Services. Raw data acquisition via Telemetry interface is well-scoped as an inbound dependency, not a diffusion. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and localized to persistence layer coordination (external data store interface) and schema evolution governance. No evidence of DP scattering across multiple modules. Recommended alternative placements (NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation) all score poorly (0.30–0.45 containment, 0.55–0.70 diffusion) due to violation of separation of concerns and would create unnecessary indirection or coupling. Safety Services is the natural, unambiguous owner.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define persistence layer interface contract: specify read/write semantics, consistency model (eventual vs. strong), latency SLAs, and data lifecycle ownership (archival, purging, backup). Document whether persistence is owned by Safety Services or delegated to external provider with clear interface boundaries.

Add schema versioning and backward compatibility policy to DP4. Specify how schema evolution is managed (e.g., version tags in normalized model, migration rules for audit trail) to prevent breaking changes to query API or audit trail interpretation.

Clarify Telemetry inbound interface validation contract: define acceptable input formats, error handling for malformed data, and fallback behavior (e.g., reject invalid features, log to audit trail, alert consumers). Ensure validation is robust and does not corrupt normalized model.

Document query API performance requirements and caching/notification strategy: specify latency SLAs for constraint queries, consider event-driven SafetyAlert notifications for high-frequency checks, and define caching policies to reduce coupling and improve responsiveness.

Add audit trail lifecycle management to DP4: specify retention policies, archival triggers, and purging rules. Ensure lifecycle is documented and owned (by Safety Services or persistence provider) to prevent unbounded growth.

Verify Tasking bidirectional interface semantics: clarify inbound operational constraint queries (from Vehicle/Teleoperation) and outbound constraint-aware feasibility responses. Ensure interface does not create circular dependencies or allow constraint violations to propagate back to Safety Services.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the DP is logically sound and complete. However, the recommended_revisions list identifies six critical gaps that must be addressed before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Given the high safety criticality of this FR, these gaps represent unacceptable residual risk: (1) persistence layer interface contract is undefined, creating ownership ambiguity for data lifecycle; (2) schema versioning and backward compatibility are not specified, risking audit trail corruption during evolution; (3) Telemetry validation contract lacks robustness guarantees, potentially allowing invalid features to propagate; (4) query API performance and notification strategy are not defined, risking latency in safety-critical constraint checks; (5) audit trail lifecycle management is absent, risking unbounded growth and availability degradation; (6) Tasking bidirectional interface semantics are unclear, risking circular dependencies. These are not minor documentation gaps—they are architectural decisions that affect safety assurance, traceability, and operational resilience. Revising DP4 to incorporate these decisions will strengthen the design before decomposing into sub-FRs, reducing rework and ensuring sub-FR allocations inherit a robust parent design.

revise.why: DP4 must be revised to explicitly address: (1) persistence layer interface contract with read/write semantics, consistency guarantees, latency SLAs, and data lifecycle ownership; (2) schema versioning and backward compatibility policy with version tags and migration rules; (3) Telemetry inbound validation contract specifying acceptable input formats, error handling, and fallback behavior; (4) query API performance requirements with latency SLAs, event-driven SafetyAlert notification strategy, and caching policies; (5) audit trail lifecycle management with retention policies, archival triggers, and purging rules; (6) Tasking bidirectional interface semantics clarifying constraint query inbound and feasibility response outbound, with circular dependency prevention. These revisions are essential to ensure DP4 is complete, unambiguous, and safe for downstream decomposition and implementation.

assumptions: Persistence layer (database or file store) for normalized model is external to Safety Services IM or is a delegated sub-component with clear interface contract.
Telemetry inbound interface supplies well-formed geometry primitives and sensor readings; validation logic is responsible for handling malformed or incomplete inputs.
Query API is accessed by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution consumers at varying frequencies; high-frequency safety state checks require low-latency response or event-driven notification.
Audit trail is unbounded in growth and requires lifecycle management (archival, purging, retention) to maintain performance and availability.
Schema evolution is anticipated as worksite features or safety policies change; versioning and backward compatibility are necessary to prevent breaking changes.
Tasking bidirectional interface is used by Vehicle and Teleoperation to query operational constraints and receive feasibility responses; circular dependencies must be prevented.
open_risks: Persistence layer ownership ambiguity: unclear whether Safety Services owns persistence or delegates to external provider; data lifecycle (archival, purging, backup) ownership is undefined. Risk: orphaned audit trail, stale model state, or data loss.

Telemetry validation robustness: inbound interface validation contract is not specified; weak validation could allow invalid features to propagate to query API consumers, violating safety constraints. Risk: safety-critical decisions based on corrupted model state.

Query API latency: synchronous request-response implementation could saturate API during high-frequency constraint checks, delaying safety-critical decisions. Risk: real-time safety violations due to latency.

Audit trail unbounded growth: no lifecycle management (archival, purging, retention) is defined; unbounded growth could degrade query API performance or exceed storage capacity. Risk: availability degradation or data loss.

Schema evolution unmanaged: no versioning or backward compatibility policy is defined; unmanaged schema changes could invalidate audit trail or break query API contracts. Risk: audit trail corruption, consumer breakage, or safety constraint violations.

Tasking circular dependencies: bidirectional interface semantics are unclear; constraint queries and feasibility responses could create circular dependencies or allow constraint violations to propagate back to Safety Services. Risk: safety constraint bypass or deadlock.
evidence_needed: Persistence layer interface specification: document read/write semantics, consistency model (eventual vs. strong), latency SLAs, and data lifecycle ownership (archival, purging, backup). Verify with persistence provider (internal or external) that contract is feasible and compliant with safety requirements.

Schema versioning and backward compatibility policy: define version tags in normalized model, migration rules for audit trail, and validation rules for schema evolution. Verify that policy prevents breaking changes to query API or audit trail interpretation.

Telemetry inbound validation contract: specify acceptable input formats, error handling for malformed data, fallback behavior (reject, log, alert), and validation robustness guarantees. Verify with Vehicle and Teleoperation that contract is clear and implementable.

Query API performance specification: define latency SLAs for constraint queries, event-driven SafetyAlert notification strategy for high-frequency checks, and caching policies. Verify that performance targets are achievable and do not introduce safety risks.

Audit trail lifecycle management policy: specify retention policies, archival triggers, purging rules, and ownership (Safety Services or persistence provider). Verify that policy prevents unbounded growth and maintains availability.

Tasking bidirectional interface specification: clarify inbound operational constraint queries and outbound constraint-aware feasibility responses. Verify that interface prevents circular dependencies and does not allow constraint violations to propagate back to Safety Services.
